title: Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds

paragraphs.text:
## **Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds**

**Noam Rozen**[1] **Aditya Grover**[2] _[,]_[3] **Maximilian Nickel**[2] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2] 1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research 3UCLA

## **Abstract**

We are interested in learning generative models for complex geometries described via manifolds, such as spheres, tori, and other implicit surfaces. Current extensions of existing (Euclidean) generative models are restricted to specific geometries and typically suffer from high computational costs. We introduce _Moser Flow_ (MF), a new class of generative models within the family of continuous normalizing flows (CNF). MF also produces a CNF via a solution to the change-of-variable formula, however differently from other CNF methods, its model (learned) density is parameterized as the source (prior) density minus the _divergence_ of a neural network (NN). The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. Therefore, unlike other CNFs, MF does not require invoking or backpropagating through an ODE solver during training. Furthermore, representing the model density explicitly as the divergence of a NN rather than as a solution of an ODE facilitates learning high fidelity densities. Theoretically, we prove that MF constitutes a universal density approximator under suitable assumptions. Empirically, we demonstrate for the first time the use of flow models for sampling from general curved surfaces and achieve significant improvements in density estimation, sample quality, and training complexity over existing CNFs on challenging synthetic geometries and real-world benchmarks from the earth and climate sciences.

## **1 Introduction**

The major successes of deep generative models in recent years are primarily in domains involving Euclidean data, such as images (Dhariwal and Nichol, 2021), text (Brown et al., 2020), and video (Kumar et al., 2019). However, many kinds of scientific data in the real world lie in non-Euclidean spaces specified as manifolds. Examples include planetary-scale data for earth and climate sciences (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020), protein interactions and brain imaging data for life sciences (Gerber et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2012), as well as 3D shapes in computer graphics (Hoppe et al., 1992; Kazhdan et al., 2006). Existing (Euclidean) generative models cannot be effectively applied in these scenarios as they would tend to assign some probability mass to areas outside the natural geometry of these domains.

An effective way to impose geometric domain constraints for deep generative modeling is to design _normalizing flows_ that operate in the desired manifold space. A normalizing flow maps a prior (source) distribution to a target distribution via the change of variables formula (Rezende and Mohamed, 2015; Dinh et al., 2016; Papamakarios et al., 2019). Early work in this direction proposed invertible architectures for learning probability distributions directly over the specific manifolds defined over spheres and tori (Rezende et al., 2020). Recently, Mathieu and Nickel (2020) proposed to extend continuous normalizing flows (CNF) (Chen et al., 2018) for generative modeling over Riemannian manifolds wherein the flows are defined via vector fields on manifolds and computed as the solution to an associated ordinary differential equation (ODE). CNFs have the advantage that the neural

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

network architectures parameterizing the flow need not be restricted via invertibility constraints. However, as we show in our experiments, existing CNFs such as FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) and Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) can be slow to converge and the generated samples can be inferior in capturing the details of high fidelity data densities. Moreover, it is a real challenge to apply Riemannian CNFs to complex geometries such as general curved surfaces.

To address these challenges, we propose Moser Flows (MF), a new class of deep generative models within the CNF family. An MF models the desired target density as the source density minus the divergence of an (unrestricted) neural network. The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. By drawing on classic results in differential geometry by Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990), we can show that this parameterization induces a CNF solution to the change-of-variables formula specified via an ODE. Since MFs directly parameterize the model density using the divergence, unlike other CNF methods, we do not require to explicitly solve the ODE for maximum likelihood training. At test-time, we use the ODE solver for generation. We derive extensions to MFs for Euclidean submanifolds that efficiently parameterize vector fields projected to the desired manifold domain. Theoretically, we prove that Moser Flows are universal generative models over Euclidean submanifolds. That is, given a Euclidean submanifold _M_ and a target continuous positive probability density _µ_ over _M_ , MFs can push arbitrary positive source density _ν_ over _M_ to densities _µ_ ¯ that are arbitrarily close to _µ_ .

We evaluate Moser Flows on a wide range of challenging real and synthetic problems defined over many different domains. On synthetic problems, we demonstrate improvements in convergence speed for attaining a desired level of details in generation quality. We then experiment with two kinds of complex geometries. First, we show significant improvements of 49% on average over Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) for density estimation as well as high-fidelity generation on 4 earth and climate science datasets corresponding to global locations of volcano eruptions, earthquakes, floods, and wildfires on spherical geometries. Next and last, we go beyond spherical geometries to demonstrate for the first time, generative models on general curved surfaces.

## **3 Moser Flow**

Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990) suggested a method for solving the normalizing equation, that is equation 2. Their method explicitly constructs a vector field _vt_ , and the flow it defines via equation 3 is guaranteed to solve equation 2. We start by introducing the method, adapted to our needs, followed by its application to generative modeling. We will use notations introduced above.

## **5 Experiments**

In all experiments, we modeled a manifold vector field as a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) _**u** θ ∈_ X( _M_ ), with parameters _θ_ . All models were trained using Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2014), and in all neural networks the activation is Softplus with _β_ = 100. Unless stated otherwise, we set _λ_ + = 0. We used an exact calculation of the divergence div _E_ ( _**u**_ ( _**x**_ )). We experimented with two kinds of manifolds.

**Flat Torus.** To test our method on Euclidean 2D data, we used _M_ as the flat torus, that is the unit square [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] with opposite edges identified. This defines a manifold with no boundary which is locally isometric to the Euclidean plane. Due to this local isometry the Riemannian divergence on the flat

## **5.1 Toy distributions**

First, we consider a collection of challenging toy 2D datasets explored in prior works (Chen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). We scale samples to lie in the flat torus [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] and use _k_ = 1 for the positional encoding. Figure 2 depicts the input data samples, the generated samples _after_ training, and the learned distribution _µ_ ¯. In the top six datasets, the MLP architecture used for Moser Flows consists of 3 hidden layers with 256 units each, whereas in the bottom two we used 4 hidden layers with 256 neurons due to the higher complexity of these distributions. ## **5.3 Time evaluations**

To compare our method to Euclidean CNF methods, we compare Moser Flow with FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) on the flat torus. We consider a challenging density with high frequencies obtained via 800x600 images (Figure 4). We generate a new batch every iteration by sampling each pixel location with probability which is proportional to the pixel intensity. The architectures of both _**v** θ_ and the vector field defined in FFJORD are the same, namely an MLP with 4 hidden layers of 256 neurons each. To capture the higher frequencies in the image we use a positional encoding with _k_ = 8 for both methods. We used a batch size of 10k. We used learning rate of 1e-5 for Moser Flow and 1e-4 for FFJORD. We used _λ−_ = 2. Learning was stopped after 5k seconds. Figure 4 presents the results. Note that Moser Flow captures high-frequency details better than FFJORD. This is also expressed in the graph on the top right, showing how the NLL decreases faster for MF than FFJORD. Furthermore, as can be inspected in the per iteration time graph on the bottom-right, MF per iteration time does not increase during training, and is roughly 1-2 order of magnitudes faster than FFJORD iteration.

## **5.4 Earth and climate science data**

We evaluate our model on the earth and climate datasets gathered in Mathieu and Nickel (2020). The projection operator _π_ in this case is simply _π_ ( _**x**_ ) = _∥_ _**xx** ∥_[.][We parameterize] _**[ v]**[θ]_[as an MLP with] 6 hidden layers of 512 neurons each. We used full batches for the NLL loss and batches of size 150k for our integral approximation. We trained for 30k epochs, with learning rate of 1e-4. We used _λ−_ = 100. The quantitative NLL results are reported in Table 1 and qualitative visualizations in 5. Note that we produce NLL scores smaller than the runner-up method by a large margin.

## **5.5 Curved surfaces**

We trained an SDF _f_ for the Stanford Bunny surface _M_ using the method in Gropp et al. (2020). To generate uniform ( _ν_ ) and data ( _µ_ ) samples over _M_ we first extract a mesh _M[′]_ from _f_ using the Marching Cubes algorithm (Lorensen and Cline, 1987) setting its resolution to 100x100x100. Then, to randomly choose a point uniformly from _M[′]_ we first randomly choose a face of the mesh with probability proportional to its area, and then randomly choose a point uniformly within that face. For target _µ_ we used clamped manifold harmonics to create a sequence of densities with increased complexity. To that end, we first computed the _k_ -th eigenfunction of the Laplace-Beltrami operator over _M[′]_ (we provide details on this computation in the supplementary), for the frequencies (eigenvalues) _k ∈{_ 10 _,_ 50 _,_ 500 _}_ . Next, we sampled the eigenfunctions at the faces’ centers, clamped their negative values, and normalized to get discrete probability densities over the faces of _M[′]_ . Then, to sample a point, we first choose a face at random based on this probability, and then random a point uniformly within that face. We take 500k i.i.d. samples of this distribution as our dataset. We take _**v** θ_ to be an MLP with 6 hidden layers of dimension 512. We use batch size of 10k for both the NLL loss and for the integral approximation; we ran for 1000 epochs with learning rate of 1e-4. We used _λ−_ = _λ_ + = 1. Figure 6 depict the results. Note that Moser Flow is able to learn the surface densities for all three frequencies.

## **6 Related Work**

In the following, we discuss related work on normalizing flows for manifold-valued data. On a high level, such methods can be divided into _Parametric_ versus _Riemannian_ methods. Parametric methods consist of a normalizing flow in the Euclidean space R _[n]_ , pushed-forward onto the manifold through an invertible map _ψ_ : R _[n] →M_ . However, to globally represent the manifold, _ψ_ needs to be a homeomorphism implying that _M_ and R _[n]_ are topologically equivalent, limiting the scope of that

approach. Existing methods in this class are often based on the exponential map exp _x_ : _TxM_ = _[∼]_ R _[n] →M_ of a manifold. This leads to the so called _wrapped_ distributions. This approach has been taken, for instance, by Falorsi et al. (2019) and Bose et al. (2020) to parametrize probability distributions on Lie groups and hyperbolic space. However, Parametric methods based on the exponential map often lead to numerical and computational challenges. For instance, in compact manifolds (e.g., spheres or the SO(3) group) computing the density of _wrapped_ distributions requires an infinite summation. On the hyperboloid, on the other hand, the exponential map is numerically not well-behaved far away from the origin (Dooley and Wildberger, 1993; Al-Mohy and Higham, 2010).

In contrast to Parametric methods, Riemannian methods operate directly on the manifold itself and, as such, avoid numerical instabilities that arise from the mapping onto the manifold. Early work in this class of models proposed transformations along geodesics on the hypersphere by evaluating the exponential map at the gradient of a scalar manifold function (Sei, 2011). Rezende et al. (2020) introduced _discrete Riemannian_ flows for hyperspheres and torii based on Möbius transformations and spherical splines. Mathieu and Nickel (2020) introduced _continuous_ flows on general Riemannian manifolds (RCNF). In contrast to discrete flows (e.g., Bose et al., 2020; Rezende et al., 2020), such time-continuous flows alleviate the previous topological constraints by parametrizing the flow as the solution to an ODE over the manifold (Grathwohl et al., 2018). Concurrently to RCNF, Lou et al. (2020) and Falorsi and Forré (2020) proposed related extensions of neural ODEs to smooth manifolds. Moser Flow also generates a CNF, however by limiting the flow space (albeit, not the generated distributions) it allows expressing the learned distribution as the divergence of a vector field.

## **7 Discussion and limitations**

We introduced Moser Flow, a generative model in the family of CNFs that represents the target density using the divergence operator applied to a vector valued neural network. The main benefits of MF stems from the simplicity and locality of the divergence operator. MFs circumvent the need to solve an ODE in the training process, and are thus applicable on a broad class of manifolds. Theoretically, we prove MF is a universal generative model, able to (approximately) generate arbitrary positive target densities from arbitrary positive prior densities. Empirically, we show MF enjoys favorable computational speed in comparison to previous CNF models, improves density estimation on spherical data compared to previous work by a large margin, and for the first time facilitate training a CNF over a general curved surface.

One important future work direction, and a current limitation, is scaling of MF to higher dimensions. This challenge can be roughly broken to three parts: First, the model probabilities _µ_ ¯ should be computed/approximated in log-scale, as probabilities are expected to decrease exponentially with the dimension. Second, the variance of the numerical approximations of the integral � _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_ will increase significantly in high dimensions and needs to be controlled. Third, the divergence term, div( _u_ ), is too costly to be computed exactly in high dimensions and needs to be approximated, similarly to other CNF approaches. Finally, our work suggests a novel generative model, and similarly to other generative models can be potentially used for generation of fake data and amplify harmful biases in the dataset. Mitigating such harms is an active and important area of ongoing research.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Moser Flow, a novel generative modeling approach for manifolds that avoids ODE solving during training by parameterizing densities via divergence. While theoretically sound and showing impressive empirical results, the method has significant scalability concerns and lacks comparison with recent simulation-free competitors.

The paper presents a theoretically elegant solution to manifold-based generative modeling with strong empirical results on low-dimensional manifolds. The mathematical derivation is rigorous and the connection to Moser's theorem is well-established. However, the scalability to high dimensions, which is acknowledged as a limitation, appears to be a fundamental bottleneck that undermines the method's practical utility for complex real-world applications.

Overview issues:

- Scalability concerns in high dimensions: The paper acknowledges high-dimensional scaling as a limitation but does not sufficiently address how this impacts the method's practical utility. The divergence computation becomes prohibitively expensive in high dimensions, and the integral approximation variance grows exponentially. The experiments are limited to 2D torus, sphere (2D manifold), and bunny surface (2D manifold embedded in 3D). No results are shown for higher-dimensional manifolds (e.g., SO(3) or higher), which are common in real applications. The claim that the method works for 'general curved surfaces' is only demonstrated on a single 2D surface example.

- Missing comparison with Riemannian Flow Matching: The paper compares against FFJORD and Riemannian CNFs but omits comparison with Riemannian Flow Matching (RFM) from Chen & Lipman (2023), which directly addresses the simulation requirement that MF aims to avoid. RFM is simulation-free and designed for scalability on manifolds, making it the most relevant baseline. Without this comparison, the claimed computational advantages of MF over existing methods remain unsubstantiated relative to the state-of-the-art.

- Hyperparameter sensitivity not thoroughly explored: The paper shows λ affects the consistency between learned and generated densities (Figure 3) but provides little guidance on selecting λ. The values used vary dramatically across experiments (λ=2 for toy problems, λ=100 for earth science data, λ=1 for bunny). The theoretical condition λ≥1 is insufficient for practical application. A sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies with λ across different manifolds and dimensionalities would strengthen the method's usability.

- Approximation bias in integral estimation: The Monte Carlo approximation of ∫_M μ_- dV using uniform samples may introduce significant bias, especially for complex manifolds where uniform sampling is non-trivial. The paper uses uniform distribution η for approximation but does not analyze how this choice affects convergence or accuracy. For manifolds where uniform sampling is difficult (e.g., implicit surfaces), this could be a practical limitation. The variance of this estimator in high dimensions is not analyzed.

- Limited evaluation on complex topologies: Despite claiming applicability to 'general curved surfaces', experiments only cover simple topologies (torus, sphere, bunny). The method's performance on manifolds with holes, boundaries, or complex topology (e.g., multiple connected components) is not evaluated. The theoretical assumptions require boundaryless manifolds, but many practical applications involve boundaries. The paper does not discuss how the method would handle or be adapted for such cases.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous universality: The paper proves a universality theorem (Theorem 3) but never demonstrates that this result has bite through a concrete example. A theory paper claiming universal approximation should include at least one worked example showing how the method succeeds where simpler approaches fail. For instance, the paper could compute the Moser Flow solution for a simple target density on the sphere (e.g., a von Mises-Fisher distribution with known concentration) and verify that the learned density converges to the target. This would help readers understand how the divergence parameterization works in practice and confirm that the approximation error can indeed be made arbitrarily small as the theorem claims.

- No worked special case for divergence computation: While the paper provides the general formula for divergence on manifolds, it never works through a concrete example showing the actual computation. This is crucial for understanding the practical implementation. The authors should compute the divergence term explicitly for a simple case like the unit sphere, showing how the projection operator and neural network combine to produce the final divergence. This would serve as a reference implementation and help verify that the theoretical divergence matches the Euclidean divergence when using infinitesimally constant vector fields, as claimed in Lemma 2.

- Underdeveloped practical guidance for manifold selection: The paper claims applicability to 'general curved surfaces' but provides minimal guidance on how to choose or represent the manifold structure in practice. The experiments only cover cases where the manifold is given analytically (sphere) or via SDF (bunny). For practitioners working with real data, the paper should discuss how to estimate the manifold structure from data, what properties the manifold must satisfy (e.g., smoothness, boundary conditions), and how errors in manifold estimation affect the generative model. This is particularly important given the assumption in the calibration notes that the manifold structure is known or accurately estimated.

- Missing inference discussion for high-dimensional settings: The paper acknowledges high-dimensional scaling as a limitation but provides no concrete guidance on inference in these settings. For a method claiming practical relevance, the authors should discuss how to implement the divergence approximation in high dimensions, what convergence rates to expect for the Monte Carlo integral estimator, and how to mitigate the curse of dimensionality. This could include specific suggestions like using importance sampling for the integral approximation or stochastic trace estimators for divergence computation, along with theoretical or empirical justification for these choices.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comparison with Riemannian Flow Matching (RFM) to substantiate computational claims

- Demonstrate scalability with experiments on higher-dimensional manifolds (at least 3D+)

- Provide analysis of hyperparameter λ selection and sensitivity across different tasks

- Include evaluation on manifolds with more complex topologies or boundaries

- Analyze the variance and bias of the integral approximation method in higher dimensions

comments.title: Division by zero in vector field definition
comments.quote:
The time derivative of this interpolant, i.e., $\frac{d}{dt}\alpha_{t}=\mu-\nu$, does not depend on $t$. Therefore the vector field can be chosen to be constant over time, $u_{t}=u$, and the PDE in equation 6 takes the form

$\operatorname{div}(u)=\nu-\mu,$ (10)

and consequently $v_{t}$ takes the form

$v_{t}=\frac{u}{(1-t)\nu+t\mu}.$ (11)
comments.explanation:
The vector field definition $v_t =  rac{u}{(1-t)
u + t\mu}$ in equation 11 is undefined when the denominator vanishes. For the linear interpolant $\alpha_t = (1-t)
u + t\mu$, there exists $t^* \in [0,1]$ such that $\alpha_{t^*}(x) = 0$ for some $x \in \mathcal{M}$ whenever $
u$ and $\mu$ have disjoint supports. This creates a singularity in the vector field. Add the condition 'Assume $\alpha_t > 0$ for all $t \in [0,1]$ and $x \in \mathcal{M}$' after equation 9 to ensure the denominator never vanishes.

comments.title: Boundary condition in Lemma 1 is insufficient
comments.quote: If $\mathcal{M}$ has no boundary, or $\bm{u}|_{\partial\mathcal{M}}\in\mathfrak{X}(\partial\mathcal{M})$, then $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}dV=1$.
comments.explanation: The condition $\bm{u}|_{\partial\mathcal{M}}\in\mathfrak{X}(\partial\mathcal{M})$ only ensures tangency but not vanishing of the boundary term in Stokes' theorem. For $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\operatorname{div}(\bm{u})dV = \int_{\partial\mathcal{M}}\langle\bm{u},\mathbf{n}\rangle dS$ to equal zero, we need either $\bm{u}|_{\partial\mathcal{M}}=0$ or the boundary integral to vanish. Rewrite Lemma 1 as: "If $\mathcal{M}$ has no boundary, or $\bm{u}|_{\partial\mathcal{M}}=0$, then $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}dV=1$" because this ensures the boundary term in Stokes' theorem vanishes.

comments.title: Positivity constraint may violate normalization
comments.quote: Since $\bar{\mu}>0$ is an open constraint and is not directly implementable, we replace it with the closed constraint $\bar{\mu}\geq\epsilon$, where $\epsilon>0$ is a small hyper-parameter.
comments.explanation: Replacing $\bar{\mu}>0$ with $\bar{\mu}\geq\epsilon$ and defining $\bar{\mu}_{+}=\max\{\epsilon,\bar{\mu}\}$ may violate the normalization property. If $\bar{\mu}$ is below $\epsilon$ on a set of positive measure, then $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV > 1$, breaking the probability density requirement. The paper needs to show that $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV = 1$ or renormalize appropriately. Add: "Note that $\bar{\mu}_{+}$ may not integrate to 1; in practice we renormalize by dividing by $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV$ when computing the likelihood."

comments.title: Loss equivalence condition is insufficient
comments.quote:
Lemma 1 and equation 13 imply that $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV=\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+1$. Therefore, an equivalent loss to the one presented in equation 14 is:

$\ell(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)+\lambda_{-}\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+\lambda_{+}\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV$ (16)

with $\lambda_{-}+\lambda_{+}\geq 1$.
comments.explanation: Using the constraint $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV=\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+1$, we substitute into equation 16: $\ell(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)+\lambda_{-}\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+\lambda_{+}(\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+1) = -\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)+(\lambda_{-}+\lambda_{+})\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+\lambda_{+}$. For this to equal equation 14 (which has coefficient 1 on the integral term and no constant term), we need $\lambda_{-}+\lambda_{+}=1$ and $\lambda_{+}=0$. The condition $\lambda_{-}+\lambda_{+}\geq 1$ is insufficient for equivalence.

comments.title: Incorrect SDF projection formula
comments.quote:
To model vector fields on an implicit surface we follow the general equation 18, where for SDFs

$$
\pi (\boldsymbol {x}) = \boldsymbol {x} - f (\boldsymbol {x}) \nabla f (\boldsymbol {x}), \quad \text {and} \quad \boldsymbol {P} _ {\boldsymbol {x}} = \boldsymbol {I} - \nabla f (\boldsymbol {x}) \nabla f (\boldsymbol {x}) ^ {T}.
$$
comments.explanation: The projection formulas for SDFs are mathematically incorrect. For a signed distance function, the gradient ∇f(x) has unit norm, but the formulas as written don't account for this properly. The correct projection onto the zero level set should be π(x) = x - f(x)∇f(x)/‖∇f(x)‖, and the projection matrix onto the tangent space should be P_x = I - ∇f(x)∇f(x)^T/‖∇f(x)‖². While for true SDFs ‖∇f(x)‖ = 1 almost everywhere, the formulas should be written in their general form to be mathematically precise.

comments.title: Ambiguous sampling procedure description
comments.quote: Then, to randomly choose a point uniformly from  $\mathcal{M}'$  we first randomly choose a face of the mesh with probability proportional to its area, and then randomly choose a point uniformly within that face.
comments.explanation: The description of uniform sampling is mathematically incorrect. Choosing a face with probability proportional to its area and then uniformly sampling within that face does NOT produce a uniform distribution over the surface. For true uniform sampling, you need to account for the fact that larger faces have more area but the same number of sampling opportunities as smaller faces. The correct procedure is to sample faces uniformly (not area-proportionally) and then sample points within faces, or alternatively use area-proportional face selection but adjust the within-face sampling density. The current method over-samples points from smaller faces.

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rate comparison
comments.quote: We used learning rate of 1e-5 for Moser Flow and 1e-4 for FFJORD.
comments.explanation: The learning rates differ by an order of magnitude between methods (1e-5 vs 1e-4), which could significantly affect convergence speed and final performance. Since the paper claims MF is faster and captures high-frequency details better, this learning rate discrepancy introduces a confounding variable. The comparison would be more convincing if both methods used the same learning rate or if the authors provided justification for why different learning rates are necessary.

comments.title: Projection operator well-definedness
comments.quote: We denote by $\pi:\mathbb{R}^{d}\to\mathcal{M}$ the closest point projection on $\mathcal{M}$, i.e., $\pi(\bm{x})=\min_{\bm{y}\in\mathcal{M}}\left\|\bm{x}-\bm{y}\right\|$
comments.explanation: Restrict the domain of $\pi$ to the tubular neighborhood $\mathcal{T}_\rho = \{\bm{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d : \text{dist}(\bm{x}, \mathcal{M}) < \rho\}$ where $\rho$ is less than the injectivity radius of $\mathcal{M}$. Rewrite as: 'We denote by $\pi:\mathcal{T}_\rho \to \mathcal{M}$ the closest point projection, which is uniquely defined for $\bm{x}$ within distance $\rho$ of $\mathcal{M}$.' This ensures $\pi$ is well-defined and smooth.

comments.title: Vector field tangency condition insufficient
comments.quote: By construction, for $\bm{x}\in\mathcal{M}$, $\bm{u}(\bm{x})\in T_{\bm{x}}\mathcal{M}$.
comments.explanation: The tangency condition $\bm{u}(\bm{x}) \in T_{\bm{x}}\mathcal{M}$ for $\bm{x} \in \mathcal{M}$ is necessary but not sufficient for the flow to remain on $\mathcal{M}$. The ODE $\dot{\bm{x}} = \bm{u}(\bm{x})$ could still leave $\mathcal{M}$ if $\bm{u}$ is not infinitesimally constant in normal directions. Add: 'Furthermore, the infinitesimal constancy in normal directions (equation 19) ensures that the flow $\Phi_t$ generated by $\bm{u}$ preserves $\mathcal{M}$ for all $t$.'

comments.title: Jacobian matrix notation inconsistency
comments.quote: where $D\Phi_{\bm{z}}$ is the matrix of partials of $\Phi$ at point $\bm{z}$, $(D\Phi_{\bm{z}})_{ij}=\frac{\partial\Phi^{*}}{\partial z^{j}}(\bm{z})$.
comments.explanation: The notation $(D\Phi_{\bm{z}})_{ij}=\frac{\partial\Phi^{*}}{\partial z^{j}}(\bm{z})$ is inconsistent. The asterisk in $\Phi^{*}$ is defined earlier as the pull-back operation on differential forms, not as a coordinate index. The standard Jacobian matrix entry should be $(D\Phi_{\bm{z}})_{ij}=\frac{\partial\Phi^{i}}{\partial z^{j}}(\bm{z})$ where $\Phi^{i}$ denotes the i-th coordinate component of the map $\Phi$.

comments.title: Loss function expectation is undefined
comments.quote: $\ell(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)+\lambda\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}(x)dV$
comments.explanation: The expectation $\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)$ is not well-defined mathematically since $\mu$ is only known through samples $\mathcal{X}=\{x_{i}\}_{i=1}^{m}$. The notation $\mathbb{E}_{\mu}$ implies integration with respect to the unknown target density $\mu$, but we only have empirical samples. Rewrite the loss as: $\ell(\theta)=-\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x_i)+\lambda\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}(x)dV$ to reflect that the first term is estimated from data samples.

comments.title: Theorem 2 condition needs clarification
comments.quote: For $\lambda\geq 1$ and sufficiently small $\epsilon>0$, the unique minimizer of the loss in equation 14 is $\bar{\mu}=\mu$.
comments.explanation: The condition "sufficiently small $\epsilon>0$" is imprecise. Earlier in the text, the requirement is $\min_{x\in\mathcal{M}}\mu(x)>\epsilon$, which depends on the target density $\mu$. Theorem 2 should state this condition explicitly rather than using the vague "sufficiently small $\epsilon$". Rewrite Theorem 2 as: "For $\lambda\geq 1$ and $\epsilon < \min_{x\in\mathcal{M}}\mu(x)$, the unique minimizer of the loss in equation 14 is $\bar{\mu}=\mu$."

comments.title: Inconsistent torus representation
comments.quote: We scale samples to lie in the flat torus  $[-1,1]^2$  and use  $k = 1$  for the positional encoding.
comments.explanation: The representation of the flat torus as $[-1,1]^2$ is mathematically ambiguous. A flat torus is typically represented as $[0,1)^2$ with periodic boundary conditions, or equivalently as the quotient space $\mathbb{R}^2/\mathbb{Z}^2$. While $[-1,1]^2$ with periodic boundary conditions could work, this should be explicitly stated. Rewrite as "We scale samples to lie in the flat torus represented as $[-1,1]^2$ with periodic boundary conditions" to clarify the topological structure.

comments.title: Inconsistent density notation in Figure 3 description
comments.quote: Figure 3 shows, for different values of  $\lambda$ , our density estimation  $\mu_{+}$ , the push-forward density  $\Phi_{*}\nu$ , and their absolute difference.
comments.explanation: The text refers to the learned density as $\mu_{+}$, but the figure caption and subsequent text use $\bar{\mu}_{+}$ for the same quantity. This inconsistency creates confusion about whether these are different quantities or just notation variants. Since $\bar{\mu}_{+}$ is the positive part of $\bar{\mu}$ (the learned density), the text should use consistent notation throughout. Rewrite the quoted sentence as "Figure 3 shows, for different values of $\lambda$, our density estimation $\bar{\mu}_{+}$, the push-forward density $\Phi_{*}\nu$, and their absolute difference."

comments.title: Inconsistent lambda notation
comments.quote: We used  $\lambda_{-} = \lambda_{+} = 1$ .
comments.explanation: The notation $\lambda_{-} = \lambda_{+} = 1$ is inconsistent with the loss function formulation in equation 14, which uses a single parameter $\lambda$ (not $\lambda_{-}$ and $\lambda_{+}$). The paper's loss function has the form $\mathcal{L} = D(\mu,\bar{\mu}_{+}) + \lambda \int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV$. Using $\lambda_{-}$ and $\lambda_{+}$ suggests two separate parameters, but only one parameter appears in the theoretical formulation. Rewrite "We used  $\lambda_{-} = \lambda_{+} = 1$" as "We used  $\lambda = 1$" to match the notation in equation 14.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Moser Flow, a conceptually novel approach to generative modeling on manifolds by leveraging the divergence-based formulation of density transport. It makes a strong contribution by eliminating the need for ODE solvers during training, which significantly improves computational efficiency over existing CNFs. The theoretical foundation is well-grounded in classical differential geometry, and the empirical results on spherical and curved surface data are compelling, especially the demonstration on the Stanford Bunny—a non-trivial manifold. The method is clearly motivated and the universality result for submanifolds is a solid theoretical achievement.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguous divergence computation on manifolds with non-Euclidean metrics: The paper relies heavily on computing the divergence of a neural vector field, but it does not clearly specify how this is done for general Riemannian manifolds beyond submanifolds of $\mathbb{R}^d$. Equation 7 defines the Riemannian divergence using the covariant derivative, yet in practice (e.g., Section 5.1 and 5.5), the authors use the Euclidean divergence $\mathrm{div}_E$, justified only for submanifolds via Lemma 2. This creates ambiguity: are the experiments on the flat torus and sphere implicitly relying on extrinsic geometry? For the flat torus, the local isometry to $\mathbb{R}^2$ justifies $\mathrm{div} = \mathrm{div}_E$, but this should be explicitly stated. More critically, the theoretical framework in Section 3 assumes a general Riemannian manifold, but the implementation and universality proof (Theorem 3) are restricted to Euclidean submanifolds. This disconnect risks misleading readers about the generality of the method. The paper should clarify that the current implementation is intrinsic only for flat or embedded manifolds with induced metrics, and that extending to general Riemannian manifolds (e.g., hyperbolic space) would require computing Christoffel symbols or other geometric quantities not discussed.

- Unjustified Monte Carlo approximation of the integral term in high curvature or dimension: The loss function includes an integral over $\mathcal{M}$ of $\bar{\mu}_-$ and $\bar{\mu}_+$, approximated via Monte Carlo sampling from a uniform distribution $\eta$. While this is practical, the paper does not address how the choice of $\eta$ affects convergence, especially when $\mathcal{M}$ has high curvature or when the target density $\mu$ is concentrated in low-volume regions. On a sphere, uniform sampling may suffice, but on a highly curved surface like the Stanford Bunny, uniformity in ambient space does not imply uniformity with respect to the Riemannian volume form. The Marching Cubes mesh used for sampling may introduce discretization bias, and the paper provides no ablation on mesh resolution or sampling strategy. Moreover, in high dimensions, the variance of the Monte Carlo estimate could dominate training, yet the paper defers this to "future work" without empirical validation of current stability. The integral approximation should be better justified, perhaps with a control variate or importance sampling scheme, or at least with a discussion of its error relative to the curvature of $\mathcal{M}$.

- Missing verification that $\bar{\mu} > \epsilon$ in practice: The method enforces $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$ via the $\bar{\mu}_-$ penalty, but the paper does not report whether this constraint is actually satisfied in experiments. Figure 3 shows $|\bar{\mu}_+ - \Phi_*\nu|$, suggesting a discrepancy between the modeled density and the generated one, which may indicate $\bar{\mu}_- > 0$ in regions. If $\bar{\mu}$ dips below $\epsilon$, the model density is no longer positive, violating the assumptions of Corollary 1 and potentially breaking the CNF construction. The authors should report the empirical value of $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$ across training and confirm it approaches zero, especially for large $\lambda$. Without this, it's unclear whether the trained model truly corresponds to a valid Moser flow or merely approximates one. This is not just a numerical detail—it strikes at the theoretical validity of the method as implemented.

- Overstated novelty in handling general curved surfaces: The paper claims to demonstrate "for the first time" generative modeling on general curved surfaces using CNFs. However, this claim is overstated. While prior work may not have applied CNFs to the Stanford Bunny, the method here still relies on the surface being embedded in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with a known SDF and projection operator. The core machinery—using $\pi(x)$ and $P_x$—only applies to Euclidean submanifolds, not to abstract Riemannian manifolds (e.g., quotient spaces, Lie groups, or manifolds without an embedding). The literature context notes that Matching Normalizing Flows and SoftFlow already address manifold-valued data, and Riemannian CNFs have been applied to spheres and tori. The novelty is better framed as an efficient divergence-based parameterization for *embedded* manifolds, not a general solution for arbitrary manifolds. The paper should temper its claims and clearly delineate the scope: embedded, compact, orientable submanifolds with computable projections.

- Lack of comparison to intrinsic baselines on curved surfaces: The experiments compare Moser Flow to mixture models and stereographic projections on spherical data, but on the Stanford Bunny, no comparison is made to alternative methods. The paper states that "no prior work has applied CNFs to general curved surfaces," but this absence of competition weakens the empirical contribution. At minimum, a baseline such as kernel density estimation on the mesh (Pelletier, 2005) or a simple histogram over faces should be included to contextualize the learned density. Additionally, the use of "clamped manifold harmonics" as ground truth is synthetic and may not reflect real-world data structure. Without a baseline, it's unclear whether the model is learning meaningful structure or simply fitting noise. The paper should include at least one intrinsic baseline or ablation (e.g., a constant density model) to validate that the improvement is not trivial.

- No verification that learned density is positive: The method requires $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon > 0$ to ensure the model corresponds to a valid CNF (Corollary 1), enforced via the $\bar{\mu}_-$ penalty in the loss. However, the paper does not report whether this constraint is actually satisfied in practice. Figure 3 shows discrepancies between $\bar{\mu}_+$ and $\Phi_*\nu$, suggesting $\bar{\mu}_-$ may not vanish. Readers will wonder if the trained model truly defines a probability density or merely approximates one. This matters because if $\bar{\mu} < \epsilon$ in regions, the theoretical link to a valid flow breaks. The authors should plot $\min_x \bar{\mu}(x)$ or $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$ over training for key experiments, especially with large $\lambda$, to confirm the penalty drives $\bar{\mu}_-$ to zero.

- Missing ablation on Monte Carlo sampling quality: The integral $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$ is estimated via uniform Monte Carlo samples on $\mathcal{M}$, but the paper does not assess the accuracy or variance of this approximation. On the Stanford Bunny, samples are drawn from a Marching Cubes mesh, which discretizes the surface—readers will question how mesh resolution affects the integral estimate. A natural question is whether poor sampling distorts training, especially in high-curvature regions. This step requires justification because the loss depends on this integral. The authors should add an ablation in Table 1 or Figure 6 showing how NLL and generated samples change with mesh resolution (e.g., 50³ vs. 100³ vs. 200³) or compare uniform sampling to area-weighted sampling on the mesh.

- No comparison to baseline on curved surface: The paper claims high-fidelity generation on the Stanford Bunny but provides no baseline to judge performance. The target is a synthetic density from clamped eigenfunctions, so it's unclear whether the model learns structure or just fits the data. This claim needs support: without a comparison, the result lacks context. A minimal baseline would be a histogram over mesh faces or kernel density estimation using geodesic distances. Even a constant density model would help calibrate whether the NLL improvements are meaningful. The authors should compute the likelihood of one such baseline on the test data and include it in Figure 6 or a supplementary table.

- Universality not demonstrated in practice: The paper proves Moser Flows are universal approximators on Euclidean submanifolds (Theorem 3) but does not show this property in action. A worked example would help readers assess the capacity of the model. For instance, can the network recover a known non-uniform density like a von Mises–Fisher mixture on the sphere? The current synthetic examples (e.g., clamped harmonics) are complex but not parametrically specified. Computing $\bar{\mu}$ for a known target $\mu$ and showing $\|\bar{\mu} - \mu\|_\infty < \epsilon$ for small $\epsilon$ would demonstrate the theorem has practical bite. The authors should fit MF to a spherical Gaussian with known parameters and report the $L^\infty$ error on a fine grid.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the scope of the divergence computation: explicitly state that the current implementation is valid only for embedded submanifolds with induced metrics, and distinguish this from general Riemannian manifolds.

- Provide empirical validation that $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$ is satisfied in practice, including plots or statistics of $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$ during training.

- Improve the justification of the Monte Carlo integral approximation, including discussion of sampling bias, mesh resolution effects, and potential variance issues.

- Temper claims about "first application to general curved surfaces" and instead position the contribution as an efficient method for embedded manifolds with projection operators.

- Add at least one intrinsic baseline or ablation study on the Stanford Bunny experiments to validate the model's performance beyond qualitative visualization.

comments.title: Unjustified Monte Carlo approximation of integral term
comments.quote:
The integral $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV$ in the losses in equation 16 and 14 is approximated by considering a set $\mathcal{Y}=\{y_{j}\}_{j=1}^{l}$ of i.i.d. samples according to some distribution $\eta$ over $\mathcal{M}$ and taking a Monte Carlo estimate

$\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV\approx\frac{1}{l}\sum_{j=1}^{l}\frac{\bar{\mu}_{-}(y_{j})}{\eta(y_{j})}.$ (17)
comments.explanation: The Monte Carlo estimator in equation (17) is mathematically incorrect as written. The term $\eta(y_j)$ refers to a probability distribution, not a density with respect to the volume form $dV$. The correct importance sampling estimator requires the Radon-Nikodym derivative $q = d\eta / dV$, so the estimate should be $\frac{1}{l} \sum \bar{\mu}_{-}(y_j) / q(y_j)$. When $\eta$ is uniform, $q(y_j) = 1 / \mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})$, so the estimator becomes $\frac{\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})}{l} \sum \bar{\mu}_{-}(y_j)$. The current form omits $\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})$, introducing a bias unless the volume is normalized. The paper must either include this factor or clarify how the volume is handled in practice, especially on curved surfaces like the Stanford Bunny where $\mathrm{Vol}(\mathcal{M})$ is not trivial.

comments.title: Missing verification that $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$ in practice
comments.quote: As expected, higher values of  $\lambda$  lead to closer modeled density  $\bar{\mu}_{+}$  and  $\Phi_{*}\nu$ . This is due to the fact that a higher value of  $\lambda$  leads to a lower value of  $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}$ , meaning  $\bar{\mu}$  is a better representation of a probability density.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that increasing $\lambda$ reduces $\int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV$, ensuring $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$, but it does not report empirical values of this integral during training. Figure 3 shows $|\bar{\mu}_{+} - \Phi_{*}\nu|$, but this does not confirm whether $\bar{\mu}_{-}$ actually vanishes. If $\bar{\mu}$ dips below $\epsilon$, the model density is no longer positive, violating the assumptions of Corollary 1 and potentially breaking the link to a valid CNF. The authors should plot $\int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV$ over training for key experiments, particularly with large $\lambda$, to demonstrate that the penalty drives $\bar{\mu}_{-}$ to zero. Without this, it's unclear whether the trained model corresponds to a valid flow or merely approximates one.

comments.title: Overstated novelty in handling general curved surfaces
comments.quote: Next and last, we go beyond spherical geometries to demonstrate for the first time, generative models on general curved surfaces.
comments.explanation: The claim of demonstrating generative modeling on 'general curved surfaces' for the first time is overstated. While the application to the Stanford Bunny is novel, the method still relies on the surface being embedded in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with a known SDF and projection operator. The core machinery—using $\pi(x)$ and $P_x$—only applies to Euclidean submanifolds, not to abstract Riemannian manifolds like hyperbolic spaces or Lie groups. Prior work such as Matching Normalizing Flows and SoftFlow already address manifold-valued data, and Riemannian CNFs have been applied to spheres and tori. The novelty is better framed as an efficient divergence-based parameterization for embedded manifolds, not a general solution for arbitrary manifolds. The claim should be tempered to reflect this scope.

comments.title: No comparison to baseline on curved surface
comments.quote: Note that Moser Flow is able to learn the surface densities for all three frequencies.
comments.explanation: The paper claims high-fidelity generation on the Stanford Bunny but provides no baseline to judge performance. The target is a synthetic density from clamped eigenfunctions, so it's unclear whether the model learns meaningful structure or just fits the data. A minimal baseline—such as kernel density estimation on the mesh or a histogram over faces—would help contextualize the learned density. Even a constant density model would calibrate whether the NLL improvements are meaningful. The authors should compute the likelihood of such a baseline on the test data and include it in Figure 6 or a supplementary table to validate that the improvement is not trivial.

comments.title: Incorrect use of $\mu_{+}$ instead of $\bar{\mu}_{+}$
comments.quote: Figure 3 shows, for different values of  $\lambda$ , our density estimation  $\mu_{+}$ , the push-forward density  $\Phi_{*}\nu$ , and their absolute difference.
comments.explanation: The text refers to the estimated density as $\mu_{+}$, but this contradicts the paper's own notation. The model outputs $\bar{\mu}$, and its clamped version is $\bar{\mu}_{+} = \max(\epsilon, \bar{\mu})$. Using $\mu_{+}$ incorrectly suggests it is a transformation of the target density $\mu$, rather than the model's estimate. This creates confusion between the true and learned densities. The label should be corrected to $\bar{\mu}_{+}$ for consistency with equations (13) and (14).

comments.title: Ambiguity in flat torus parametrization
comments.quote: We scale samples to lie in the flat torus  $[-1,1]^2$  and use  $k = 1$  for the positional encoding.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'scale samples to lie in the flat torus $[-1,1]^2$' is ambiguous because $[-1,1]^2$ is a square in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not a torus. To form a flat torus, opposite edges must be identified periodically, which is not stated. Without this identification, the domain has boundaries, violating the compact, boundaryless assumption required for Lemma 1 and Corollary 1. The authors should clarify that periodic boundary conditions are enforced, either through the positional encoding or explicitly in training, to ensure the geometric structure aligns with the theoretical framework.

comments.title: Incorrect sign in generalized KL expansion
comments.quote:
$D(\mu,\bar{\mu}_{+})$ $=\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\left(\frac{\mu}{\bar{\mu}_{+}}\right)-\int_{\mathcal{M}}\mu dV+\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV$
$=\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\mu-\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}+\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV$
comments.explanation: The second equality in the generalized KL expansion is correct, but the derivation is not shown. The step $\int \bar{\mu}_{+} dV - \int \mu dV = \int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV$ relies on Lemma 1 and equation (13), which state $\int \bar{\mu}_{+} dV = \int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV + 1$ and $\int \mu dV = 1$. However, this substitution is not made explicit, making the derivation appear unjustified. Readers will wonder where $\int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV$ comes from. The paper should insert the intermediate step: 'Using Lemma 1, $\int_{\mathcal{M}} \bar{\mu}_{+} dV = \int_{\mathcal{M}} \bar{\mu}_{-} dV + 1$, so $\int \bar{\mu}_{+} dV - \int \mu dV = \int \bar{\mu}_{-} dV$.'

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rates confound runtime comparison
comments.quote: We used learning rate of 1e-5 for Moser Flow and 1e-4 for FFJORD. We used  $\lambda_{-} = 2$ . Learning was stopped after 5k seconds. Figure 4 presents the results. Note that Moser Flow captures high-frequency details better than FFJORD. This is also expressed in the graph on the top right, showing how the NLL decreases faster for MF than FFJORD.
comments.explanation: The comparison of training speed and NLL convergence between Moser Flow and FFJORD uses different learning rates—1e-5 for MF and 1e-4 for FFJORD—without justification. A tenfold higher learning rate for FFJORD may cause instability or poor conditioning, artificially slowing its progress, while the lower rate for MF could allow smoother descent. Stopping training after a fixed wall-clock time (5k seconds) conflates algorithmic efficiency with implementation speed. If FFJORD's per-iteration cost is higher due to ODE solvers, the difference in runtime is expected, but this does not imply MF is inherently more efficient. The observed NLL improvement could stem from unequal optimization budgets. The comparison should use matched learning rates or normalize by FLOPs to ensure fairness.

comments.title: Mesh discretization conflated with continuous manifold
comments.quote: To generate uniform  $(\nu)$  and data  $(\mu)$  samples over  $\mathcal{M}$  we first extract a mesh  $\mathcal{M}'$  from  $f$  using the Marching Cubes algorithm (Lorensen and Cline, 1987) setting its resolution to  $100 \times 100 \times 100$ .
comments.explanation: The paper treats the mesh $\mathcal{M}'$ as equivalent to the true surface $\mathcal{M}$, but $\mathcal{M}'$ is a piecewise-linear approximation constructed at finite resolution. This introduces discretization error in sampling, eigenfunction computation, and training. The theoretical framework assumes a smooth Riemannian manifold, but all operations are performed on $\mathcal{M}'$, whose geometry differs from $\mathcal{M}$. For example, the Laplace-Beltrami eigenfunctions used to define $\mu$ are computed on $\mathcal{M}'$, not $\mathcal{M}$, so the target density is not defined on the original manifold. The authors should clarify that experiments are conducted on $\mathcal{M}'$ and acknowledge that results depend on mesh resolution.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Moser Flow (MF), a continuous normalizing flow approach for generative modeling on manifolds. By parameterizing the model density as the divergence of a neural network, the authors bypass the need for solving ODEs during training, offering a significant computational advantage over existing Riemannian CNFs.

Moser Flow provides a elegant and computationally efficient framework for manifold-constrained generative modeling. The theoretical grounding in Moser's theorem is sound, and the empirical results on complex surfaces demonstrate clear performance gains. This is a strong contribution that effectively addresses the bottleneck of ODE integration during training.

Overview issues:

- Unclear handling of the divergence-free component: In Section 3.2, the model density is defined as $\bar{\mu} = \nu - \operatorname{div}(u)$. While Lemma 1 ensures the integral of $\bar{\mu}$ is 1 under specific boundary conditions, the construction relies on the existence of a vector field $u$ whose divergence matches the target density. The paper does not address the existence of a Helmholtz-like decomposition for the vector field $u$ on arbitrary manifolds. Specifically, it is unclear if the space of divergence-based densities $\bar{\mu}$ is sufficiently expressive to cover all target distributions $\mu$ without requiring $u$ to have a non-trivial curl component that could lead to instabilities or non-uniqueness in the flow $\Phi_1$. The authors should clarify if the parameterization $\operatorname{div}(u)$ restricts the reachable manifold of densities compared to a general CNF.

- Numerical stability of the divergence operator in high dimensions: The authors acknowledge in Section 7 that exact computation of $\operatorname{div}(u)$ becomes costly in high dimensions. However, the paper proceeds to use exact calculations for all experiments. The transition from exact divergence to an approximation (e.g., Hutchinson's trace estimator) is not theoretically analyzed in the context of the loss function $\ell(\theta)$ in Equation 14. If the divergence is approximated, the gradient of the loss $\ell(\theta)$ would involve the gradient of the divergence estimator, which can be highly noisy. A discussion or preliminary experiment on the variance of these gradients in higher-dimensional settings would be necessary to support the claim that this method scales effectively.

- Potential for probability mass leakage: The loss function in Equation 14 uses a penalty term $\lambda \int_{\mathcal{M}} \bar{\mu}_- dV$ to enforce the positivity constraint $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$. In practice, this integral is approximated via Monte Carlo sampling (Equation 17). On complex manifolds or near boundaries, the uniform distribution $\eta$ used for sampling may not provide sufficient coverage of the manifold, leading to regions where $\bar{\mu} < \epsilon$ go undetected. This could result in the model 'leaking' probability mass, where the density becomes negative or zero in unobserved regions, violating the diffeomorphism requirement of the flow $\Phi_1$. The authors should provide a more robust analysis of how the choice of sampling density $\eta$ affects the validity of the resulting flow.

- Topological constraints and global diffeomorphism: Theorem 2 and Theorem 3 claim universality for Moser Flows on submanifolds. However, the existence of a global diffeomorphism $\Phi$ between the prior $\nu$ and target $\mu$ assumes that the manifold is globally diffeomorphic to the reference space. While the paper assumes a compact, orientable, connected manifold, it does not explicitly address potential topological obstructions or singularities that might arise if the target density $\mu$ has a support that is not simply connected or possesses non-trivial homology. A brief discussion on how the vector field $u$ behaves under such topological constraints would strengthen the theoretical claims.

- Empirical validation of divergence approximation for high-dimensional manifolds: The paper claims scalability to high-dimensional manifolds but relies on exact divergence calculations in all experiments. Since exact computation is often intractable in high dimensions, the authors should provide a simulation comparing the exact divergence with a stochastic approximation, such as the Hutchinson trace estimator, on a high-dimensional manifold like a hypersphere in 50+ dimensions. This would demonstrate that the gradient noise from the approximation does not prevent convergence and that the proposed loss function remains stable when the divergence is estimated rather than computed exactly.

- Demonstration of Moser Flow on non-simply connected manifolds: The theoretical universality results assume a compact, connected manifold, but the paper does not demonstrate how the model handles manifolds with non-trivial topology, such as a torus with multiple holes or a higher-genus surface. To confirm the model's robustness, the authors should compute the Moser Flow for a target density on a genus-2 surface (e.g., a double torus). This would clarify whether the vector field parametrization successfully captures densities on surfaces that are not globally diffeomorphic to a simple sphere or plane.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a discussion on the variance of the gradient when using stochastic divergence estimators for higher-dimensional manifolds.

- Clarify the impact of the sampling distribution $\eta$ on the positivity constraint $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$ in regions with sparse data.

- Add a brief theoretical note on the limitations of the divergence-based parameterization regarding the Helmholtz decomposition of vector fields on non-trivial topologies.

comments.title: Inconsistency in loss function equivalence
comments.quote:

Lemma 1 and equation 13 imply that $\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV=\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+1$. Therefore, an equivalent loss to the one presented in equation 14 is:

$\ell(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\log\bar{\mu}_{+}(x)+\lambda_{-}\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV+\lambda_{+}\int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{+}dV$ (16)

with $\lambda_{-}+\lambda_{+}\geq
comments.explanation: The claim that equation 16 is equivalent to equation 14 is not supported by the provided identity. Substituting \int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1 into the penalty terms of equation 16 yields (\lambda_- + \lambda_+) \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + \lambda_+. For this to be equivalent to the penalty term in equation 14, one would require \lambda_- + \lambda_+ = \lambda and the constant \lambda_+ to be independent of \theta. However, since \bar{\mu}_+ depends on \theta, the term \lambda_+ \int \bar{\mu}_+ dV$ is not constant unless \lambda_+ = 0. Please clarify if equation 16 is a modified objective or provide a derivation showing why the gradient of the additional term vanishes.

comments.title: Inconsistency in integral approximation notation
comments.quote: \int_{\mathcal{M}}\bar{\mu}_{-}dV\approx\frac{1}{l}\sum_{j=1}^{l}\frac{\bar{\mu}_{-}(y_{j})}{\eta(y_{j})}.$ (17)
comments.explanation: The estimator provided is the standard importance sampling formula. If \eta is the uniform distribution, then \eta(y) = 1/\text{Vol}(\mathcal{M}) for all y \in \mathcal{M}. Substituting this into Equation 17 yields \frac{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{M})}{l} \sum_{j=1}^{l} \bar{\mu}_{-}(y_j). The current expression \frac{1}{l} \sum \frac{\bar{\mu}_{-}(y_j)}{\eta(y_j)} is misleading because it suggests \eta is a function that varies with y_j. Please rewrite Equation 17 to explicitly account for the uniform density.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding training complexity
comments.quote: Therefore, unlike other CNFs, MF does not require invoking or backpropagating through an ODE solver during training.
comments.explanation: This claim is misleading. While the density estimation step is bypassed by the divergence-based parameterization, the generation of samples from the model still requires integrating the vector field v_t via an ODE solver. The authors should clarify that this efficiency gain applies only to the density estimation phase of training, not the generative sampling process.

comments.title: Inclusion of absolute value in pullback formula
comments.quote:
